turns.content: hey man quick question. if you were running a small auto shop and had some cash coming in steady, would you say its smarter to invest in the location you got or try to grow into a second spot

That's a great question, and it really depends on a few things. The biggest factors are usually:

1. **How maxed out is your current location?** If you're turning away work because you don't have the bays or the hours, that's a sign expansion could make sense.
2. **How clean is your balance sheet?** Opening a second location typically means taking on significant new debt — lease, buildout, equipment, staffing — before you see a dime back.
3. **Can your current shop run without you on the floor?** A second location splits your attention, and if the first shop dips because you're not there, you end up worse off.

What's got you thinking about this? Are you at capacity where you are now?
well I had been looking at a spot in Germantown actually. nice area, good traffic, seemed like a no brainer for a while

Germantown is a solid area — good demographics, people with money who take care of their vehicles. I can see why that would be appealing for an auto shop.

What's the situation with the space? Are you still exploring it, or have you gotten into the details like lease terms, buildout costs, that kind of thing?
yeah the Germantown thing is off the table. ran the numbers again and it doesn't make sense, not with what I still owe on the equipment loan

Honestly, that sounds like a smart call. A lot of small shop owners get caught up in the excitement of growing and don't weigh the existing debt load against the new expenses. Taking on a second location while you're still paying down equipment is risky — one slow month and you're stretched thin in two places instead of one.

The fact that you ran the numbers and made a clear-eyed decision says a lot. That's discipline, not retreat.

Does that mean you're thinking about doubling down on the current shop instead? Sometimes the better play is making what you've got more profitable.
thats exactly what Im thinking. I got some equipment coming in that should help me take on more work without the overhead of a whole second building

That's the move. New equipment at your existing location gives you more revenue capacity without doubling your rent, utilities, insurance, and staffing costs. The margin on additional work at a shop you already operate is way better than the margin on the same work at a brand new location.

What kind of equipment are you bringing in? Depending on what it is, there might be some ways to market the new capabilities and drive traffic to it.
alignment rack. been sending that work out for too long. time to keep it in house

That's a huge one. Alignments are steady money — good margins, repeat business, and they pair naturally with tire work. Every tire sale becomes a potential alignment upsell, and customers love a one-stop shop.

Once you've got it installed and dialed in, I'd recommend letting your existing customer base know. A simple text or a sign in the shop that says "Now offering alignments" can fill up that rack fast. People who've been going somewhere else for alignments would probably rather keep everything with a shop they already trust.

How soon are you expecting delivery?
couple months out still. but Im already planning for it. gonna need to get one of my guys trained up on it too

Smart to think about training now rather than when the equipment shows up. A couple of things to look into:

- The equipment manufacturer usually offers training as part of the purchase or for a small fee. That's your best first step since they know the machine inside and out.
- Hunter, Hofmann, John Bean — whoever makes your rack — most of them have online certification courses too that your tech can start before the equipment even arrives.

The learning curve on modern alignment racks is honestly not bad. If your guy is solid on suspension and steering work, he'll pick it up quick. The software does a lot of the heavy lifting now.

Worth the investment to get him trained right from day one so you're not fumbling through the first few weeks of jobs.
appreciate it man. I feel better about the decision to stay put and build up what I got. Germantown would have been cool but it would have had me losing sleep

That peace of mind is worth something. Growing smart beats growing fast every time. You're adding revenue with the alignment rack, keeping your debt manageable, and not spreading yourself thin. That's a solid position to be in.

Down the road, if the numbers change and that second location makes sense, the opportunity will come back around. Right now you're making the right call. Good luck with the equipment rollout.
